data.title: The Transformation Of Bitcoin Mining Into AI Hosting: Opportunities And Risks
data.description:
As a bitcoin (BTC-USD) investor for almost 8 years, I think I'm used to volatility. Bitcoin miners aren't immune to the volatility of their own end products. But the largest by market capitalization are now transforming their business to become AI and HPC hosting providers. Or at least, that's what they are trying to do.

The business shift comes from declining revenues from Bitcoin mining and the revenue boom generated by AI, especially thanks to the capital expenditures of hyperscalers. The problem miners are facing is the decline in Bitcoin which, after reaching an all-time high of $122,000 last October, sharply dropped to $64,000 in February.

Despite the recent rebound, the drop has been significant over the past few years. I was very surprised when I looked at which instrument performed better over the past five years: Bitcoin or the S&P500 (SPY). I thought Bitcoin was the instrument that outperformed the market. But I was wrong.

In this article I wrote why I no longer want to hold Bitcoin, and that I'm just waiting for the right moment to sell and take some profits on the investment I made in 2018. I don't want to own any more Bitcoin because I thought it would be more widely adopted in the future, especially as a medium of exchange that would start replacing the dollar bill. But it wasn't like that.

Profitability problems of crypto miners

Besides my own interpretation about bitcoin and its price, mining companies are losing revenue and profitability due to the drop in hashrate. In this case, it's not just that Bitcoin has fallen to 2024 lows that matters, but that the key profitability unit for miners is far from 2021 levels.

This is a result, in my opinion, of increased competition, which drove up energy consumption in the global mining network. We must add to this the 2024 Bitcoin halving, which, while boosting the price, didn't help miners regain profitability. In other words: Bitcoin miners' revenue per hashrate dropped from approximately $400 in 2021 to around $50 by early 2026.

At these hashprice levels, even if Bitcoin returns to its all-time highs, recovering past profitability levels seems very unlikely. Let's take into account that the fifth halving will occur in 2028, so profitability could decrease further. It's true there is also an opportunity, but the price should rise significantly more. I'll use Riot Platforms (RIOT) as an example. In their 2025 10K, on ​​slide 45, the cost per bitcoin was $91,427, yielding a 10% return. If bitcoin doesn't strongly surpass that figure, 2026 will be a challenging year for RIOT. This is just one example. Later, I'll analyze the situation of both RIOT and its peers.

With these examples, I wanted to highlight some of the reasons that could explain the shift miners are experiencing. And they're doing so with the goal of making the most of their energy capacity. After all, crypto mining, as any other business, relies on generating higher revenues than costs. Energy costs weren't necessarily the problem for miners: it was their declining revenue. That's where the opportunity arose to join the AI ​​boom, which requires a lot of energy to operate.

The AI ​​transition factor

Basically, the simple explanation is that AI demand was so strong in the last three years that data centers, historically needed for HPC, went from measuring their power in kilowatts to megawatts and gigawatts. So, more energy from all sources will be needed, and much of that energy was previously used for cryptomining. Next, I'll show you a chart that I found very telling about this boom.

The image was taken from an investor presentation by Bitfarms (KEEL), a miner that is transitioning into an AI data center company, which will now be called Keel Infrastructure. The change of the curve is very evident. In 2022, kilowatt contracts rose 15%, the highest increase since 2006 (green line).

With increased energy demand, AI data centers are becoming key to the infrastructure that supports modern computing. And a big part of the investment needed by AI is directed toward energy. It doesn't matter if the language models for AI are for inference or training, if they use chips from Nvidia (NVDA) or Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), or if they use memory from Micron Technology (MU) or Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) All data centers need energy with a lot of power. That's why I really like this type of investment. It's a picks-and-shovels investment. By 2030, data centers could be the largest consumer of electricity globally, second only to three countries.

Which bitcoin mining companies are driving this transformation?

By the end of 2026, Bitcoin miners could derive nearly 70% of their revenue from supplying AI data centers. But there are some differences in the sector. Some miners want to become major players due to contracts signed with hyperscalers for cloud services. In almost every case, those contracts are backed by plans to build large data centers and projections of high growth in installed capacity. To put in order the players of this transformation, I ranked the companies by installed capacity in the following table.

I took the main 10 players per market cap. Not all of them have the same progress nor the same goals. The mining companies that already have partnerships with hyperscalers are IREN, HUT, CIFR, WULF, and CORZ. Those pivoting with significant contracts are RIOT, KEEL, APLD, MARA, and CLSK. Every earnings call, management talks about progress in their transition. This is something I find really interesting, even without concrete contracts yet.

To sum up, there is a lot of variety in the business plan of each company. So, in the following lines I'll focus on providing a brief overview of each company's situation. Because although I like them all as investment opportunities, they are not all moving at the same pace. Besides, I believe it's very important to evaluate purchase prices and profitability. The most common thing will be to see net losses and low returns, as in any transition. However, not all companies lose at the same rate or share the same trend.

Summary of each company: expansion projects and profitability

I'll follow the order of the previous chart. IREN has the largest installed capacity (4.5 GW) and will present its Q3 FY2026 results on May 13. In the previous quarter, the net loss was $155 million, with revenues of $185 million. Adjustments to non-recurring monetary items can distort the net income analysis. I believe Adjusted EBITDA could be a more useful metric to analyse earnings. In this case, IREN had an adjusted EBITDA of $75 million against the $62 million of the same period the previous year.

But what makes IREN very interesting is that it counts on Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) as a key hyperscaler partner. The contract signed with MSFT is for $9.7 billion over 10 years. Moreover, the contract also helped IREN secure $3.6 billion in credit, allowing it to borrow $3.6 billion. IREN still derives 91% of its revenue from Bitcoin mining. That's why, both in the next quarter and in subsequent ones, I expect to see that percentage steadily decline.

CIFR, which has recently changed its name to Cipher Digital, is another key player with a strong installed power capacity and a contract with Fluidstack, a cloud infrastructure operator backed by Alphabet (GOOG). But CIFR has something else: Amazon (AMZN). With both contracts, it projects revenues of $9 billion. For now, CIFR's revenue is still 100% crypto mining, but I also expect to see some AI lines added soon. The earnings presentation will be in May. In 2025, CIFR's revenue was $224 million, compared to $151 million in 2024. The net loss was $822 million, driven by expenses and machine depreciation, leaving an adjusted EBITDA of $40 million.

WULF also has agreements with Fluidstack and financial backing from Google. WULF has contracted revenue of $13 billion for the next 10-25 years. Like IREN and CORZ, WULF has an HPC revenue line item on its FY2025 balance sheet. This amounted to $17 million of the total $168 million. Net losses increased from $62 million in 2024 to $661 million in 2025. Meanwhile, Adjusted EBITDA was negative by $23 million.

APLD has confidentiality agreements with its major clients. Last Thursday, the company announced a new contract for its data center Delta Forge 1, which has 430 MW but I don't know who the hyperscaler tenant is. Total revenue is estimated at $7.5 billion for the next 15 years. Wes Commins, its CEO, says he wants to achieve contracts with five big hyperscalers: Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), Oracle (ORCL), Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG). Currently, its financial statements (the latest being for Q3 FY26) show no revenue streams related to AI. Net losses in the last nine months of FY2026 were $138 million, a smaller amount than the same period the previous year, $179 million.

I see KEEL as a smaller player, but with great potential. The company expects to be a key hosting provider for NVDA's Vera Rubin GPUs, which could be commercially available by the end of this year. The campus to house Vera Rubin is Panther Creek, a data center project in Pennsylvania with the goal of exceeding 500 MW. The company had losses of $284 million in FY2025, while adjusted EBITDA was positive, at $28 million (-8% YoY).

CLSK has a contracted capacity of 1.8 GW, of which almost 1.2 GW corresponds to the States of Texas and Georgia. The company has already presented its Q1 2026 results, and both earnings and adjusted EBITDA fell into negative territory. The net loss was $378 million and the adjusted EBITDA was negative at $295 million, in line with the profitability lows seen across its peers. I believe CLSK is in an earlier phase of adaptation, and it still doesn't have any contracts or major clients to show.

In the case of RIOT, there isn't a clear hyperscaler, but it does work with AMD, with whom it signed a contract for 200 MW at the high end of the supply range. I believe Riot has an advantage, which is that it can demonstrate clear operational capacity of up to 1.8 GW, including the contract with AMD. Anyway, RIOT doesn't escape the difficult situation of Bitcoin mining, with projected losses of $663 million in 2025 (slide 34). Adjusted EBITDA remained positive at $12 million, although this was a significant decrease compared to the $463 million of 2024.

MARA has a strategic partnership with Starwood Capital Group, a specialized firm in real estate developments for hyperscalers. The agreement is estimated to achieve an installed capacity of 1 GW in the short term and reach 2.5 GW in the future. In Q4 2025 I saw a pronounced negative adjusted EBITDA of $1.4 billion, almost double the $795 million of the same period of the previous year.

HUT reported revenues of $235 million in 2025, compared to $162 million in 2024, and net losses of $248 million. From the $235 million of total income, $115 million were from bitcoin production. Only $7.4 million was generated from AI cloud services. When compared with IREN, with its 8-9% AI revenue share, HUT's revenues are only 3-4%. HUT also experienced a decline in adjusted EBITDA, with a negative result of $135 million, compared to $556 million in 2024. Although the results don't seem so great, HUT announced in December a contract backed by Google, with an agreement of $7 billion to supply 245 MW, which, like WULF and CIFR, is through Fluidstack.

CORZ has an installed capacity of 1.3 GW and supply contracts for 1.5 GW, thanks to its main partner, CoreWeave, which is also a strategic partner of NVDA. CORZ has a 12-year agreement with ARR, estimated at $850 million. The company has more diversification than others, and is present in 7 states. What I like most about CORZ is that it's already showing an HPC hosting segment called Colocation (slide 58 of the 10-K report). And that allows it to show some advantages in gross margins, which increased 19% in 2025. Net losses were $288 million, but they were less than the $1.4 billion of 2024.

My three favorite stocks

While I believe there are really interesting long term investment opportunities, I also think some of them are showing more success than others. In my case, I'm going with IREN, KEEL and RIOT. Once again: I believe those companies have very solid fundamentals to make a long term investment.

I'm choosing IREN for its capacity, trust and guarantee of having MSFT behind, an estimated ARR of $3.44 billion, and because its adjusted EBITDA shows a positive trend. Besides, I believe its progress in the transition is very solid. Although only 9% of their revenue comes from cloud AI services, that's already more than other miners. In the case of KEEL and RIOT, there isn't a strong contract in sight, but I believe their expansion projects are solid enough to supply energy to a hyperscaler. Both have a positive adjusted EBITDA, although with a declining trend. But RIOT already has AMD as a partner, while KEEL seems to point to NVDA, as I said before.

This is very interesting because the announcement of signed contracts with hyperscalers gives a boost to the share price of the leasing company, to the point where you could end up late to the game and buy at a high price. That's why RIOT and KEEL, although they haven't signed big contracts, are cheap options to take advantage of.

Investment Risks: volatility, valuations and funding

One of the main risks of investing in crypto mining is, still, volatility. Since they all still depend heavily on Bitcoin, both its price and the daily hashrate per unit, the AI infrastructure business still looks far away. If there is no concrete progress by 2026, for example, with all companies adding a line item related to AI to their balance sheets, I think it could generate distrust in the markets.

As with companies that transform their business, uncertainty is higher, and therefore, so is the potential reward. I believe valuations represent another investment risk because markets reward future income very quickly without considering direct competition, among other factors. The case of IREN shares, for example, which reached all-time highs at the end of 2025 (around $76) and, almost six months ago, were unable to strongly break above $60. The risk would be buying the best performers at the wrong price.

Finally, funding through share issuance can hurt shareholder confidence. In my case, I'm closely following RIOT's share issuance, which slowed down in 2025 compared to previous years. I believe this is a risk to keep a close eye on for any company in the sector.

Conclusions

The lower Bitcoin mining profitability in recent years has pushed many miners to shift toward hosting AI and HPC workloads, taking advantage of the large investments that hyperscalers are making in data centers. Basically, the goal is to use their existing energy infrastructure and turn it into a more profitable business.

I tried to show an overview of the sector using the top 10 publicly traded Bitcoin mining companies and analyze their differences. As with any investment in naturally volatile assets, there are underlying risks, and I believe we must take them into account, among them high valuations and financing methods. I am choosing just three companies (IREN, KEEL, and RIOT). I am open to changing my portfolio in the medium term. However, given my enthusiasm for the future of electricity demand, I believe any of these 10 companies could serve as exposure to the sector.

data.title: Merchants Bancorp: Earnings Continue To Show Upcoming Headwinds
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, Indiana-based Merchants Bancorp (MBIN) reported its first quarter earnings. The stock has been on a tear, outperforming the S&P 500 by three times since I downgraded the common shares back in June. While the bank beat earnings expectations and continues to experience strong revenue growth, I believe that investors are better served at this time investing in the preferred shares than the common shares.

Merchants Bancorp Earnings

Merchants Bancorp’s asset yield continues to decline as short-term interest rates drop. Asset yields fell by 30 basis points in the first quarter to 6.14%. Compared to a year ago, asset yields declined by 65 basis points. Borrowing yields experienced a greater decline, dropping by 36 basis points in the first quarter to 3.64%. Compared to a year ago, borrowing yields are 77 basis points lower.

Due to the bank’s borrowing yield dropping faster than asset yields, net interest spreads have risen, going from 2.38% in the first quarter of 2025 to 2.44% in the fourth quarter and up 6 more basis points to 2.50% in the first quarter. Net interest margin, which incorporates the weight of leverage into the mix, has also risen, going up by 3 basis points in the first quarter to 2.92%, the same increase as a year ago.

When it comes to income and expenses, interest income pulled back in the first quarter, dropping by $36 million to $271 million. Interest expenses also declined, but by a little less, at $27 million to $142 million. Due to these changes, net interest income (interest income less interest expense) dropped by $9 million to $129 million. Net interest income for the bank has been essentially flat for the past two years.

Changes in Loans and Deposits

Merchants Bancorp had the best quarter for loan growth since 2023 in the first quarter. The bank grew lending by 8.8% in the first quarter. Over the last year, the bank has grown lending by an impressive pace at 12.6%. Deposit growth has been more elusive for the bank. In the first quarter, deposits declined by 0.7%, following a decline of more than 6% in the fourth quarter. Despite these drops, the two previous quarters saw robust increases strong enough to fuel positive year-over-year growth, which stood at 4.4% in the first quarter.

By outpacing deposit growth, Merchant Bancorp’s already high loan-to-deposit ratio was sent even higher in the first quarter. Loan-to-deposit ratios of over 100% among banks are considered high because at those levels, the bank needs to leverage itself further with external financing to fund loan growth. Due to the combination of loan growth and deposit declines, Merchant Bancorp’s loan-to-deposit ratio has gone from 104% to 124% in just two quarters!

The extreme change in the bank’s loan-to-deposit ratios has forced the bank to turn to external financing to fund its loan growth. External financing is more expensive than deposits, and when it is needed, like in this situation, it can erode net interest income and overall earnings. Merchants Bancorp’s external financing has gone from $2.9 to nearly $4.8 billion in just two quarters. External financing now accounts for nearly 30% of gross loans.

Risks: The Factors Keeping Me from the Common Shares

Merchants Bancorp has a loan performance problem. Following the pandemic, nonperforming assets were minuscule and under 10 basis points of gross loans. Today, they’ve steadily risen over a two-year period to around 2% of gross loans, a 20-fold increase.

The nonperforming asset problem is further complicated by the allowance for credit losses, which at under 50 basis points of gross loans, is nearly a quarter of the nonperforming assets level. The bank disclosed in the first quarter earnings release that the allowance for credit losses dropped after the bank took some hits on loans. With the allowance well below peers and its nonperforming assets, there’s no doubt that the bank will need to bolster the allowance soon, which will require a sacrifice to earnings and common share value.

The bank also disclosed that funds were added to the allowance for credit losses due to decreases in multi-family property values and ongoing investigations of borrowers for mortgage fraud. These issues are likely to perpetuate for future quarters, and I don’t want to risk my capital on shares that have experienced a big run up with these headwinds on the horizon.

Is One Preferred Share Better?

Merchants offer three distinct preferred share offerings. One preferred share (MBINN) is fixed, callable, and trading at 18% below par, with a dividend yield of 7.33%. Another is fixed to floating (MBINM), with shares set to float next October. This issue is trading at slightly over par and yielding 8.13%. The third (MBINL) is fixed and cannot be called until January of 2030. It is trading at nearly 4% below par and yielding 7.93%.

I like MBINL because of its fixed payment guarantee until January of 2030. While MBINM is paying 20 basis points more, its floating rate is set to go slightly below MBINL next October if interest rates stay the same as they are today. If you incorporate a rate cut this year and one next year, the yield could be 50 basis points or lower by next fall. With MBINL, the dividend payment is fixed and not callable.

Conclusion

Merchants’ inadequate allowance for credit losses and elevated nonperforming assets is certainly a risk to future earnings. If earnings take a hit, common shares will face the brunt of the volatility. Since preferred shares cannot be touched unless common share dividends are eliminated, I believe preferred shares are insulated by most of these issues. Investors can collect 8% income against the backdrop of their stable investment while waiting out the volatility in the bank’s common shares.

data.title: PEO: This Energy And Basic Materials Fund May Be Worth Considering Today
data.description:
The Adams Natural Resources Fund, Inc. (PEO) is a closed-end fund that investors can use as a way to receive a very high yield from their assets while having exposure to energy and materials stocks. The past month or so has shown the importance of having some exposure to energy companies, as many companies in this sector have performed incredibly well since the outbreak of the Iran War. Furthermore, we have seen a pattern over the past month in which the broad-market indices decline on any day that crude oil prices rise.

Generally speaking, energy stocks rise whenever crude oil rises. In other words, recently, energy stocks have exhibited an inverse correlation to the broad-market indices. Thus, it may make sense for an investor to increase their exposure to energy as a way of reducing the overall volatility of their portfolios. The Adams Natural Resources Fund could be a way to obtain this exposure and earn a 7.91% yield in the process.

About The Adams Natural Resources Fund

The website for the Adams Natural Resources Fund offers the following description of the fund and its strategy:

Adams Natural Resources Fund seeks to deliver superior returns over time by capitalizing on the long-term demand for energy and materials. The Fund invests in energy and natural resources stocks and seeks to generate returns that exceed its benchmark as well as consistently distribute dividend income and capital gains to shareholders.

Adams Funds is one of the oldest fund managers in the industry today, and this particular fund has a very long history as a result. The Adams Natural Resources Fund began operation back in 1929, which gives it a long track record that we can look at to evaluate the fund’s historical performance.

Adams recently claimed on its website that both of the closed-end funds that this company manages—the Adams Diversified Equity Fund, Inc. (ADX) and the Adams Natural Resources Fund—have outperformed for the past ten years:

This actually is true if we look at the total returns that the Adams Natural Resources Fund has produced over the past decade:

This chart compares the trailing ten-year performance of the Adams Natural Resources Fund to the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE). As we can see, the two funds exhibit a very high correlation with each other. However, the 180.09% total return delivered by the Adams Natural Resources Fund was better than the 160.96% total return that the Energy Select Sector Index delivered. We should note, however, that both energy funds underperformed the 311.24% return that the S&P 500 Index Total Return (SP500TR) delivered over the period. The outperformance of PEO that Adams Funds is claiming is relative to the Energy Select Sector Index, not to the S&P 500 Index. Indeed, as was mentioned in the index, energy companies have been exhibiting a negative correlation with the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the past month or two. This was also the case back in 2022, another period in which crude oil prices rose substantially:

One reason for this inverse correlation has to do with interest rates. It is no secret that for most of the past decade, the S&P 500 Index has moved inversely to interest rates, much like bonds. Whenever investors expect interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve, the S&P 500 Index moves up and vice versa. Crude oil is an input into many things that we all purchase in our regular lives, as it is used to manufacture plastics, synthetic fibers (clothing), various cosmetics and personal care products, and other things.

In addition, crude oil is used as a transportation fuel, and nearly all shipping involves the use of oil-derived products. For example, everything that is purchased in a retail store was transported there by a vehicle that runs on some sort of refined petroleum (crude oil).

Thus, in a lot of ways, oil is an input into just about every other product on the market. This means that when oil prices rise, businesses experience higher costs. The general belief is that businesses will pass on these higher costs to their customers, which ultimately leads to rising prices throughout the economy. These higher prices could prompt the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates to combat inflation, and as was mentioned, the S&P 500 Index has been exhibiting an inverse correlation with interest rates. Thus, whenever oil prices rise, it applies downward pressure on the broad-market indices and leads to the inverse correlation between energy and the rest of the market that we have been seeing.

As such, it could be a good idea for investors to have energy exposure in their portfolios, such as the exposure that can be obtained by owning shares of the Adams Natural Resources Fund. If the inverse correlation holds, then this fund should rise whenever the rest of the market falls. This should help the volatility in a portfolio because its gains will partially offset the losses suffered by other companies’ common stocks whenever oil prices rise on a given day.

The description provided on the website for the Adams Natural Resources Fund suggests that this fund invests in both energy companies, such as most oil and natural gas companies, as well as natural resources companies, such as those in the mining industry. However, as of March 31, 2026, the bulk of the fund’s holdings were actually in energy companies. Here they are:

None of these companies are miners, as all of them are considered to be part of the energy industry. However, not all of them actually produce crude oil and natural gas. The Williams Companies, Inc. (WMB) and Targa Resources Corp. (TRGP), for example, make their money by transporting crude oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids from place to place. In the case of those two companies, the resources that are transported are mostly natural gas and natural gas liquids, but the fund’s most recent schedule of investments states that it had positions in both Kinder Morgan, Inc. (KMI) and ONEOK, Inc. (OKE) on March 31, 2026. Kinder Morgan and ONEOK both have crude oil and refined products transportation businesses.

In addition to pipeline operators and hydrocarbon transportation companies, we see Phillips 66 (PSX), Valero Energy Corporation (VLO), and Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC), which also do not actually extract crude oil from the ground. Rather, PSX, VLO, and MPC are refining and marketing companies in that they take crude oil and turn it into gasoline, jet fuel, and other oil-derived products that are actually used by energy consumers. All three companies also actively supply branded gas stations where people can park their cars and fill up their tanks, but in most cases, these gas stations are not actually owned by Marathon Petroleum, Valero, or Phillips 66.

We also see SLB N.V. (SLB) among the fund’s largest positions, which also does not actually extract crude oil from the ground. Rather, SLB is an oilfield services company that performs seismic testing, reservoir testing, well construction, and software design for companies that actually produce crude oil and natural gas. All of these companies play a vital role in the oil and gas industry, even though not all of them actually produce crude oil or natural gas.

While all the companies listed in the fund’s largest positions list are either producers of crude oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids or are connected with the energy industry in some way, the Adams Natural Resources Fund is not exclusively an energy sector fund. There are some mining companies in the fund’s portfolio as well. The most recent schedule of investments includes the following list:

These companies only accounted for 4.6% of the fund’s net assets as of March 31, 2026, so they are not going to have a huge impact on the fund’s performance. This could be unfortunate, as some of these companies could be very strong performers going forward.

In two recent articles (see here and here), I stated that gold miners could have a great deal of potential upside as they are significantly undervalued given today’s price of gold. Newmont Corporation (NEM) is one company that falls into this category, as it is currently trading with a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 11.34, and the models used by analysts for calculating that figure may be underestimating the amount of money that Newmont receives for each ounce of gold that it sells. For example, about a year ago, Morningstar stated that it expected gold miners to realize an average price of $3,170 per ounce through 2027 and expected a mid-cycle gold price of $2,000 per ounce from 2029. That is far below the current gold price (which has been bouncing between $4,600 and $4,800 per ounce for the past few weeks), and it is also well below the $4,900 per ounce that Newmont realized on average during the first quarter of 2026.

Newmont also announced a $6 billion share buyback on top of the existing buyback. As Newmont only has a market capitalization of $123.92 billion, this positions the company to buy back at least 4.84% of the current shares outstanding unless the share price rises going forward. This should put a strong tailwind on the share price that pushes it upward.

Furthermore, I strongly disagree with Morningstar’s conviction that gold prices will average $2,000 per ounce after 2029. The fiscal outlook of most governments around the world seems likely to keep gold prices elevated for the foreseeable future as institutions and private individuals seek to preserve their purchasing power in the face of fiscal dominance. It is, admittedly, debatable whether Newmont will outperform the rest of the gold mining sector, but it is very easy to see a bullish case for it, and that will likely benefit the Adams Natural Resources Fund.

It is worth noting that many analysts here on Seeking Alpha are bullish or, at worst, neutral on Newmont. The company has buy ratings from analysts on Seeking Alpha, on Wall Street, and in Seeking Alpha’s Quant Valuation system:

Out of all the articles that were published on Newmont since August 1, 2022, only one of them has the author assigning a “Sell” rating. Thus, there appears to be a great deal of consensus that this is a good materials stock to own, and I agree with the consensus.

Cameco Corporation (CCJ) is another mining stock that is held in the portfolio of the Adams Natural Resources Fund that may have a bright future. Cameco is the largest publicly traded uranium mining company in the world, and as such, its business is largely tied to nuclear power. After all, with the exception of a few scientific labs, the only organizations that purchase uranium in large quantities are electric utilities that own nuclear power plants and governments that own submarines and naval vessels with nuclear reactors.

Despite this limited customer base, Cameco could be positioned for growth. As I stated in a recent article, there is likely to be a surge in electrical consumption going forward. The technology industry’s aggressive build-out of data centers to support artificial intelligence is a big reason for this, as it requires an enormous amount of electricity to make artificial intelligence technology work. According to Yale University, artificial intelligence data centers will consume approximately 945 TWh of electricity by 2030. That represents a doubling from 2025 levels, and it is slightly more than the entire electricity consumption of the nation of Japan. Obviously, meeting the needs for that level of electrical consumption would require that an enormous amount of generation capacity be added to the grid. While some policymakers and technology executives have proposed that renewable power supply this need, most acknowledge that renewables cannot do it with today’s technology. A data center consumes electricity 24 hours a day, seven days a week, which poses problems for both solar and wind generation. After all, solar power does not work very well if the sun is not shining (such as at night), and wind power does not work well if the air is still. Nuclear power is the only carbon-free source of energy available today that can produce sufficient electricity on a constant basis to satisfy the needs of data centers. The International Energy Agency appears to agree with this assessment. According to an article on World Nuclear News, global nuclear generation capacity will rise to 728 GWe by 2050, a 73.33% increase from the 2024 level. Nuclear power requires uranium, and as Cameco is the largest publicly traded uranium producer in the world, it is easy to see how it could potentially benefit from this trend.

While both Newmont and Cameco have some very real potential to deliver capital gains going forward, both companies only account for a relatively small proportion of the fund’s assets. As of March 31, 2026, the fund had $13,899,300 invested in Newmont, which was 1.60% of its total assets as of the same date. The fund’s position in Cameco was even smaller, as its $1,954,980 total holdings only accounted for 0.23% of the fund’s total assets. Thus, while the fund will benefit if those stocks exhibit strength going forward, it does not have enough exposure to them to really drive the share price upwards. Rather, it seems likely that it will be the energy stocks that drive the fund’s performance for the time being, as 83.1% of the fund’s net assets were invested in energy stocks at the end of March. Let us take a look at the fundamentals for that sector.

Energy Fundamentals And Outlook

The past fifteen months or so have been something of a roller coaster for crude oil prices. This chart shows the spot price of West Texas Intermediate crude oil since the start of 2025 through today:

As we can see, the spot price of West Texas Intermediate crude oil has been in a general downtrend since the start of 2025. There were two exceptions to this. The first exception occurred during the middle of June 2025, and the second exception started about two months ago and continues through today. The reasons for both exceptions, as well as the general downtrend, are largely political. In January 2025, President Trump began his administration in the United States. Mr. Trump is generally considered to be fairly friendly towards the American oil and gas industry, and one of his campaign promises is that he would encourage oil and gas companies operating in the various U.S. shale plays to increase their production in an effort to bring down the price of crude oil. The oil and gas industry in the United States was indeed steadily growing production throughout 2025, although it is unclear how much a role Mr. Trump’s policies actually played in this process. With that said, though, there was minimal crude oil production growth between late 2023 and 2025, but American oil production surged from January 2025 through October before declining again:

It is not atypical for production to decline in the last few months of the year. If we look at the five-year production chart, we can see month-over-month declines in either November, December, or January in most years:

Regardless of the cause, American production of crude oil was generally increasing over the course of 2025. The price of oil is generally a function of supply and demand. If production rises more quickly than consumption, the price of oil will decline. As we just saw, the production of crude oil in the United States was generally rising in 2025, and it set an all-time high in October. The United States was not the only country growing its production in 2025, however. The member nations of the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries were also growing their production over the course of 2025. This chart shows the average daily production for the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries for every month since November 2021:

As we can see, over the period from 2021 through late 2024, OPEC’s daily production was generally between about 28.5 million and 29.5 million barrels of oil per day. That increased in 2025, with most of the months recording figures in the 29.5 million to 31.4 million barrels per day range. Thus, we have a combination of rising American production and rising production from the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries. This rising supply was pressuring prices downward over the period. Thus, we ended up with steadily declining oil prices over the course of 2025.

As mentioned, there were two exceptions to the downtrend in oil prices that we have seen since the start of 2025. The first was in June 2025, which was caused by a joint US-Israel military strike against select targets in Iran. The price of oil spiked because traders were concerned that the Iranians might retaliate against the strikes and that this retaliation could include a closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which is one of the most important water passages for crude oil in the world. This is because the Strait of Hormuz is the only way for oil tankers to get out of the Persian Gulf and into the wider ocean. As it happens, though, the Iranians did not retaliate, and the countries involved moved straight to peace talks. This calmed the commodity markets, and oil prices returned to their previous downtrend.

The second exception to the downtrend is the one that is still going on today. As with the first occurrence, this one was also caused by a series of joint US-Israel military strikes against Iran. In this case, though, the Iranians did retaliate and ultimately managed to close passage through the Strait of Hormuz. On a typical day, oil tankers carrying around 20.3 million barrels of oil in total pass through the Strait. That represents somewhere between 20% and 25% of all oil consumed worldwide. Obviously, once passage through the Strait of Hormuz was closed off, this oil could no longer leave the Persian Gulf and reach end users (primarily in Europe, Eastern Asia, and Oceania). This oil still exists, but the fact that it cannot get to consumers means that it is still sufficient to cause a supply shortage. As oil prices follow the economic law of supply and demand, this supply shortage caused oil prices to rise. This has continued through to the present day.

The fact that the recent surge in oil prices is based on geopolitical factors makes it difficult to predict when prices will come back down. One aspect of Iran’s retaliation for the military strikes was to use drones and missiles to hit oil production sites throughout the Middle East. In addition to this, crude oil storage sites throughout the Middle East are filled to very close to their maximum capacity. As such, some oil companies operating in the Middle East have been forced to shut down some of their production locations. The damage to production infrastructure and the facility shutdowns mean that the supply of oil to the rest of the world will not immediately go back to its previous levels once the Strait of Hormuz has been reopened.

Goldman Sachs recently came out and predicted that the price of Brent crude oil will remain above $80 per barrel until at least 2028:

We can see that the bank’s base case assumes that crude oil flows out of the Persian Gulf return to just below their pre-war levels by the end of June, and even that puts Brent crude oil at $80 per barrel at the end of 2027. Goldman’s base case assumes that the production levels of all the countries surrounding the Persian Gulf are 500,000 barrels per day below their pre-war levels to account for the infrastructure damage that was discussed in the previous paragraph. Even the most benign scenario still puts crude oil prices at above $60 per barrel by the end of 2027, and that assumes that shale oil drillers in the United States and in other countries around the world that have not been hit by Iranian drones and missiles boost their production sufficiently to offset the destroyed production capacity in the Middle East.

There are a lot of variables to consider when trying to make a projection about how long oil prices will remain at today’s levels. Goldman Sachs clearly believes that it will take until late 2027, assuming that oil production increases in the United States and the Strait of Hormuz is fully reopened to oceangoing traffic by mid-June. We are seeing evidence that American oil producers are increasing their production. Earlier this month, Continental Resources stated that it will be increasing its capital spending budget, which will increase its production and allow the company to take advantage of higher oil prices. As of the time of writing, Continental Resources is the only company that has announced that it will attempt to increase its production, but it is certainly possible that some other companies are having such discussions internally. The most recent rig count shows that the number of drilling rigs actively drilling wells in the United States is down week-over-week:

This does not point towards American oil companies making efforts to boost their production at this time, but once again, it is possible that some upstream oil producers are having this discussion internally.

The futures market appears to be priced for a quick reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and possibly an increase in U.S. oil production. Presently, West Texas Intermediate crude oil is priced at $78.73 per barrel for December 2026 delivery and $70.73 for December 2027 delivery:

Thus, the futures market is also saying that prices will remain above their pre-war levels for quite some time. Goldman Sachs thinks that these prices are too low given the current macroeconomic and geopolitical backdrop. In either case, though, it does look like crude oil producers will have higher profits over the next several quarters than they reported in 2025. The Adams Natural Resources Fund has 83.1% of its net assets invested in the common equities of energy companies. If indeed Goldman Sachs is correct, then many of these energy companies are undervalued right now. If so, then it seems likely that the energy stocks held in this fund will appreciate, and that would increase the returns that PEO can provide to its investors.

Distribution Analysis

The Adams Natural Resources Fund does not market itself as an income investment. Unlike many funds on the market, the fund does not pay a stable monthly dividend. Rather, it has a managed distribution plan under which the fund pays out 8% of its net asset value on an annualized basis. The fund pays a quarterly distribution, so this results in the fund paying out whatever amount works out to 2% of its net asset value. The fourth-quarter distribution might sometimes be larger if the fund had a good year and needs to distribute some of its gains to satisfy the tax rules surrounding investment companies. The result of this is that the fund’s distribution will vary from quarter to quarter:

As we can see, the fund’s quarterly distribution has ranged from $0.4800 to $0.5400 per share since it implemented its current distribution policy in mid-2024. We can expect that the variable distribution will continue going forward, which may reduce the fund’s appeal according to those investors for whom the receipt of stable income is a priority. As the fund is only paying out 2% of its net asset value quarterly, though, it should allow the distribution to be sustainable over the long term. After all, common equities in general return more than 8% per year.

With that said, we should still take a look at the fund’s finances to determine how well it is actually doing. As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report that is available for this fund is the annual report for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025. Let us take a look at it and see how the fund did at covering its distribution for the most recent year.

For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, the Adams Natural Resources Fund reported a net investment income of $16,270,848. This was not sufficient to cover the $54,794,247 that the fund paid out over the same period.

Fortunately, the fund was able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, the Adams Natural Resources Fund reported net realized gains of $41,858,264, but this was partially offset by net unrealized losses of $3,367,544. The fund’s investment portfolio produced total profits of $54,761,568 for the year:

At first glance, it may appear that this was not enough to fully cover the payout. After all, the fund’s distribution was $32,679 higher than the change in net assets from operations. However, we need to keep in mind that unrealized losses can be erased by strength in the market. As such, we can ignore them when determining whether a fund is overdistributing. The sum of the net investment income and net realized gains was $58,129,112. That was sufficient to cover the distribution with money left over.

This fund simply distributed its net realized profits. That is the ideal situation, and it is pretty much what we like to see.

Valuation

Shares of the Adams Natural Resources Fund are currently trading at a 9.97% discount to its most recently reported net asset value. This is slightly worse than the 11.13% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month, but it is still a very large discount.

Over the past five years, PEO has traded at prices ranging from a 7.51% discount to an 18.62% discount on net asset value. The average price over the five-year period was a 13.28% discount. Thus, the fund’s current price is above its five-year average, but anyone who purchases the fund’s shares at the current level will still effectively be obtaining its assets for a lot less than they are actually worth.

There are very few peer funds available to compare PEO with. The closest closed-end fund in terms of composition is the BlackRock Energy & Resources Trust (BGR). That fund is trading at a 7.50% discount on the most recently reported net asset value. Thus, the Adams Natural Resources Fund is the cheaper of the two funds right now.

Expenses

The website for the Adams Natural Resources Fund states that it has a 0.56% expense ratio. This is incredibly low for an actively managed fund.

Conclusion

The Adams Natural Resources Fund is a long-running closed-end fund that has managed to beat the Energy Select Sector Index over the past decade. The fund invests in a combination of energy and basic materials stocks, and both seem like a good place to be right now. It looks very likely that oil prices will remain above $70, and possibly above $80 per barrel for a while, which provides a tailwind to energy stocks. Furthermore, the aggressive build-out of artificial intelligence data centers is supportive of nuclear energy, which this fund also has some exposure to.

Given that energy stocks have been moving inversely to the rest of the market over the past two months, this fund might be worth considering as a way to reduce volatility in your portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Starbucks: This Undefeatable Coffee Champion Has Been Judged Beyond Logic
data.description:
In the months that followed my previous coverage, Starbucks Corporation (SBUX) has weakened, which revealed its sensitivity to recent macroeconomic changes. Even so, its robust fundamentals are evident and justify my positive view of this stock. Its still unmatched global market penetration and competitiveness continue to drive its growth and expansion. Its valuation is also still reasonable at this level and suggests some upside potential. The only problem now is that technicals signal bearish tendencies, although buying opportunities may emerge again after the potential dip.

SBUX Q2 2026: Unshaken Resilience Amid Market Challenges

Many restaurant businesses are facing the various challenges posed by the continued increase in prices, fueled by tariff wars and recent geopolitical tensions. Their vulnerability to market volatility may be high considering softer consumer spending and purchasing power. But amid all this, Starbucks Corporation remains well-grounded and strong. Its solid market positioning and prudent store management are also evident. These things have been reflected in its most recent performance.

In Q2 2026, SBUX sustained its strong start of FY 2026. Its operating revenue amounted to $9.53B, up by 8.8% YoY from $8.76B. This YoY increase was also much stronger than in my previous coverage at 5.5%. In fact, this was strongest performance in eight quarters or since Q3 2024. This shows its continued recovery and growth despite the impact of inflationary headwinds, labor disputes, and even boycotts.

One aspect to consider is unparalleled and unmatched brand recognition and customer loyalty. Although competition among restaurants and coffee shops remains stiff, it still appears that SBUX emerged victorious if you look at its robust revenue growth. Even its actual net fees from licensed stores also increased, which suggests that their sales also rose. Its continued expansion also drove this growth because more stores meant more sales. But what is more interesting is that its expansion is not just for the sake of increasing its store presence. After all, SBUX captured more foot traffic. If you look at its restaurant count, SBUX had a total of 21,557 company-operated restaurants globally versus 21,505 YoY. The net increase was only 52 stores. Yet, the company operated revenue increased by over $500M. If we divided the revenue by the total number of restaurants, the average revenue per company operated restaurant was $362,592 versus $338,758 YoY. This means that each restaurant has become more productive and in-demand in general.

Company-Operated Revenue Number Of Company-Operated Restaurants Average Revenue Per Restaurant
Q2 2025 $7,285,000,000 21,505 $338,758
Q2 2026 $7,816,400,000 21,557 $362,592

Likewise, its operating costs and expenses also increased in line with its increased restaurant count. Even so, the increase remained manageable at only 6.5%, so revenue growth easily offset. This showed improved efficiency despite the rising prices and store count. I also think that its prudent restaurant management helped manage its expenses. If you look at its store data, the net number of company-operated restaurant in the international segment reached 10. This could imply that SBUX closed underperforming or inefficient stores to save costs and concentrate on high-performing ones. As a result, its operating margin to 8.7% versus 6.9% YoY.

Risk Factors

As a business operating in the restaurant and coffee shop industry, SBUX is still susceptible to macroeconomic risks. Currently, inflation has already regained its momentum to its 20-month high of 3.3%. In just a month, inflation increased by 90 bps. This shows the high sensitivity of domestic prices to external aspects like tariff wars and skyrocketing oil prices amid the Middle East tension. This also shows that the Fed is still having a hard time keeping it under control. With that, discretionary spending may soften amid tighter purchasing power.

On top of that, SBUX may face increased cost pressures. The price of ingredients it uses may increase. And because oil and fuel prices are skyrocketing the cost of transporting raw materials and coffee beans may increase. Also, it may also have to raise its wages, so employees can keep up with the rising prices. Note that SBUX still faces labor disputes, which may intensify considering the macroeconomic conditions in the US. Competition and pricing inflexibility may also be a challenge to its sales and operating margins. However, I believe that the macroeconomic risks and intense competition will have unexpected positive spillovers, which I will discuss more in the next section.

Risk Mitigants And Drivers

Competition may become more predatory and appear to be in a dog-eat-dog environment. As coffee shops fight for volume/foot traffic demand, the cost of operations may keep rising. This may hurt their pricing power since they tend to capitalize on cheaper pricing to fight more expensive and popular brands with strong customer retention like Starbucks and Tim Horton's (TH). This may have two outcomes if the situation escalates. First, they may have to raise prices, so revenues can absorb extra costs. However, this may affect their demand. Note that their cheaper pricing strategies cater more to lower-income households or buyers. Second, they may opt to keep their prices unchanged to retain their volume, but this will also hurt their margins. Either of the two outcomes, smaller and local coffee shops may cannibalize one another because their cheap pricing appears to be predatory to one another.

If you ask me, SBUX couldn't care less about it. It can even take advantage of the situation. Why? Its reputation is already established, which is something one cannot build overnight. Also, it already has a defined target market who are less concerned with pricing. As you can see, its pricing strategy is at a premium but still affordable, especially by middle-income, upper-middle-income and working customers. These people are more resilient to inflation. And to retain its strong customer base, its sticky gamification strategies like select discounts and stars for free items somehow make it a good deal for them. Quality coffee and items for free for loyal customers? They can even consume them inside the shop while working. In the end, the question is about who will survive amid the intense macroeconomic environment and competition. I guess the answer will be simple - the ones with a defined and loyal customer base and with a huge capital buffer to keep going even if things become challenging. With that, I believe that SBUX will always capture a demand and remain on top.

Considering everything I explained, it's not surprising that its sales, average revenue per company-operated restaurant, and margins all expanded. This happened despite the fact that inflation reached 3.3% last March. With that, it is safe to say that many of its customers remain resilient to inflation, and that product differentiation continues to work for SBUX.

Lastly, SBUX has robust fundamentals. Its strong Balance Sheet is characterized by liquidity. Its cash and cash equivalents dropped but remained ample at $1.53B. It also has other liquid assets like investments and receivables.

SBUX complements it with prudent debt management through timely issuances and repayments. As you can see, its maturity profile remains long since over 80% of the total has a maturity of more than one year. To be more specific, 50% of the total will not mature within five years. This is still in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 2.5x, which means that SBUX only needs 2.5 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn't have to. It's leverage is still moderately low, so it still has enough buffer to sustain its expanding capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model or DDM. The annualized dividend remains at $2.48 based on the most recent quarterly payout, so the dividend growth rate is still 7.84%. Meanwhile, I still set its risk premium at 6.0% to be consistent with my previous coverage and price in more risks instead of just keeping at the same as the 5.0% market standard. This makes my estimation a bit more conservative. But note that the risk-free rate is now 4.4%, which can put some downward pressure on my TP or target price. Lastly, Beta is slightly lower at 0.95, which means that SBUX has become less volatile recently. This also shows that its price swings are more manageable than the S&P 500. In the end, my TP decreased to $109.73. However, this is still higher than the current price, which shows that the stock is still reasonably cheap today.

Meanwhile, P/S shows that the stock trades at only 2.93x its sales versus the five-year average of 3.29x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $109.18. This is very close to the DDM TP, which supports my positive view of its valuation.

Technicals

SBUX's technicals have shown recent selling pressures, although recent rebound attempts have been evident. The stock price now trends above the 50-day SMA after bouncing back from the selloff. It shows that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. But note that the trend appears sideways in the past three weeks, showing market caution. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA has already flattened, showing that market sentiments have neutralized or become cautious.

Its MACD and RSI also show the same thing. The MACD line has recently increased but appears to be decreasing again as selling volume increases again. The histogram is within positive territory, showing that buying volume outpaces selling volume. But its decreasing trend also shows that selling volume is starting to catch up with buying volume. Its RSI also reflects it due to the recent downtrend. On a lighter note, you can see that the trend of the two indicators are now consistent with the price trend. The recent selloff was due to a bearish divergence, driven by overbuying. But the selloff served as a correction. The consistency may suggest that rebound attempts still hold today. Also, note that the stock became almost oversold, which prompted its rebound. Now, there is enough buying room for investors.

Conclusion

Starbucks Corporation stays robust with sustained growth and margin expansion. Its unmatched gamification and market positioning as well as its solid fundamentals ensure stability amid its expansion in a volatile macroeconomic environment. Valuation is still reasonably cheap and shows upside potential. Technicals show selling pressures, but rebound attempts may still hold as buying opportunities remain wide. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Timberland Bancorp: Low Loan Loss Provisions Boost Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

I have been following Timberland Bancorp (TSBK) for in excess of five years now, and since that initial article, the bank's share price has doubled. A decent result, and although the total return of in excess of 100% isn't exactly hitting the ball out of the park in that time frame, I definitely like the risk/reward here. Mainly because the total amount of loan loss provisions of this Washington state focused bank remains low. This allows it to rapidly increase its tangible book value per share, which also increased by approximately 50% in the past four years.

The results remain strong

One of the key reasons why I continue to be interested in Timberland Bancorp is the bank’s strong financial results. This was once again confirmed in the December quarter (the bank hasn’t reported yet on its Q1 CY 2026 results) as the total amount of interest and dividend income increased by in excess of 7%, while the total amount of interest expenses decreased by 0.5%. This resulted in an increase of the net interest income by in excess of 10%, and that, of course, goes a long way to further boost the bottom line result.

As the image above shows, the bank remains very capable of keeping its loan loss provisions exceptionally low, and in the December quarter of 2025, the total amount of loan loss provisions was just $35,000 (less than $0.1M indeed). That's a very negligible amount given the total loan book stands at almost $1.5B.

That's important because the bank’s bottom line result mainly depends on its net interest income. The image above also shows that the total amount of non-interest income is just under $2.8M, while the total amount of non-interest expenses (shown below) came in at $11.4M.

This meant that the total pre-tax income came in at $10.3M, which is an increase of approximately 15% compared to the same quarter a year before. This also resulted in a bottom line net profit of approximately $8.2M, representing $1.04 per share. That's an increase of approximately 20% thanks to the higher net profit in combination with a slightly lower net share count.

And it goes without saying that the quarterly dividend of $0.28 per share (now increased to $0.29) remains very well covered by the earnings, considering the payout ratio remains firmly below 30%.

The loan book also remains robust

One of the key elements that explains why the bank is able to put in a very robust financial performance is the exceptionally low amount of loan loss provisions.

Let's take a step back and have another look at the bank's balance sheet. As you can see below, the total amount of assets on the balance sheet is approximately $2B, which is a very small decrease compared to the preceding quarter. It's important to see that the balance sheet is very liquid, with almost a quarter of a billion in cash and an additional $200M in securities and certificates of deposit.

But what, of course, really matters is the approximately $1.46B loan book. And in order to understand why the total amount of loan loss provisions remains this low, we need to have a closer look at the total breakdown of the loan book.

As you can see below, despite having in excess of 40% of the total amount of loans outstanding in commercial real estate, it's remarkable that only $304,000 of those loans are currently classified as "past due." This represents less than 0.1% of the CRE loans, which really indicates that the quality of underwriting is very strong.

It's somewhat surprising to see that the main problem child are they residential mortgage loans, but keep in mind those loans obviously have real estate assets as collateral, so the haircut for the bank should remain very limited.

All things considered, it's absolutely remarkable to see that on a total loan book of almost $1.5B, just under $6.1M of those loans are classified as "past due," with the vast majority of those loans being backed by collateral that could be monetized by the bank.

Investment thesis

I currently have a small long position in Timberland Bancorp, but I wish I owned more. Thanks to the bank’s local focus, it has been able to keep loan loss provisions to a very low level while the increasing net interest income is boosting the financial results.

The bank should be able to post a full-year EPS of around (or perhaps slightly in excess of) $4 (of course, barring unforeseen circumstances), so the current earnings multiple of approximately 10 still doesn't make the stock expensive.

At the end of December, the total tangible book value came in at just over $32/share, and given the strong financial performance in combination with the low dividend payout ratio (which means that it retains the majority of its earnings on the balance sheet, thereby boosting the tangible book value per share), I'm more than happy to pay a premium of 25% to 30% on the tangible book value.

I would be interested in adding to my position, but I'm in no rush and may wait for the bank’s next quarterly results to come out first.

data.title: Stanley Black & Decker: Solid Start To 2026 For This Dividend Aristocrat
data.description:
Earnings season rolls on. Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) reported solid Q1 results on Wednesday, April 29. Shares were little changed later in the morning, and the same goes when zooming out to the past few years.

I had a "Buy" rating on SWK back in October 2024. Shares are down 22% since then, lagging the S&P 500 by some 45 percentage points. But I still see value in this Industrial name with consumer exposure. With a low-teens multiple, the chart is not as desirable. I’ll detail Q1, SWK’s fundamentals and valuation, and offer a refreshed look at the chart.

SWK Has Rallied Recently, But Remains Well Below Its Early-Year High

In April, SWK reported a healthy set of quarterly results. Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $0.80 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.59, while revenue of $3.85 billion, up 3% from the same quarter a year earlier, was a notable $100 million beat. The company expects FY 2026 GAAP EPS to be in the range of $4.15 to $5.35.

Shares traded higher by just 0.3% ahead of the open after earnings. If the gain holds by the close of trading on the penultimate day of April, it would mark the third consecutive post-earnings rally. The options market had priced in a moderate 6.5% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the report. Implied volatility on SWK should now retreat from 54%, while short interest on the Industrials-sector stock is material at 4.1%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, it was a decent start to 2026 for SWK. Results appeared on track to meet the firm’s annual financial targets. Organic revenue was about flat, as a 3% increase in price was offset by a 3% decline in volume. Moreover, the company tallied adjusted EPS of $0.80 over the January through March stretch and completed the divestiture of its Consolidated Aerospace Manufacturing (CAM) business for approximately $1.6 billion, using the majority of the proceeds to reduce debt. I like to see that kind of capital-allocation discipline.

While its adjusted gross margin declined slightly by 20 basis points to 30.2% due to inflation and tariff expenses, SWK's Engineered Fastening segment saw significant margin expansion of 190 basis points, driven by a 31% surge in aerospace organic growth.

Color On Quarter

Looking ahead, SWK sees a relatively stable balance year, forecasting total net sales to be about flat with organic revenue increasing in the low single digits. It projects significant profitability improvements, too, including positive adjusted EBITDA margin growth YoY. According to the Q1 report, the progress is underpinned by an ambitious gross margin expansion plan, aiming for roughly 34% to 35% in 2H as the company works toward its long-term goal of 35% to 37%.

Following the CAM divestiture, SWK expects to end the year with a reduced leverage ratio of ~2.5x and will prioritize returning cash to shareholders through buybacks. The outlook accounts for current geopolitical tensions and a specific tariff schedule that assumes Section 122 duties remain in place through late July.

2026 Outlook

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to grow at an impressive clip over the quarters to come. The FY 2026 bottom line could rise 12%, with even better gains in the out year and through FY 2028. Of course, SWK’s operating EPS remains far below levels from 2021.

Indeed, the sellside has turned less upbeat about the company’s profit outlook. There have been a high 7 EPS downgrades over the past 90 days, compared to just a lone upgrade. I’d call out that the free cash flow yield is above 5%, although broader profitability trends leave something to be desired.

SWK: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates to my 2024 assessment. If we assume $6 of normalized non-GAAP EPS over the next 12 months and apply a below-market 15x price-to-earnings multiple, shares should trade near $90. I assert that the current low-teens forward P/E is too low, with a mid-teens bottom-line growth rate from 2027 through 2028.

Shares also trade cheap across valuation metrics, including a compressed price-to-sales ratio.

SWK: Attractive Valuation Multiples

Key risks for SWK include macroeconomic volatility from interest rates and geopolitical instability, particularly potential inflationary pressures from conflicts in the Middle East. Elsewhere, the firm faces significant trade-related exposure regarding the timing and impact of Section 232 and 301 tariffs, alongside the challenge of successfully mitigating these costs through price increases.

What’s more, performance depends on the effective execution of supply chain transformations and on new product innovations gaining market traction despite volume deleveraging and competitive pressures in the industry.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a projected Q2 2026 earnings date of Tuesday, July 28, BMO. Shares trade ex a $0.83 dividend on Monday, June 8.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares mildly undervalued following a good start to 2026, SWK’s technical situation is not all that appealing to the bulls. Notice in the chart below that the stock is little changed from three years ago. In fact, SWK is stuck in the middle of a range with a high amount of shares traded, indicating high congestion. To that point, both the short-term 50-day moving average and the long-term 200-day moving average are soon to be flat in their slopes. The stock is above those two trend-indicator lines, but not by much.

Also take a look at where support and resistance lie. There’s an uptrend line that comes into the picture at $66, while the more important line in the sand is the horizontal line in the low $90s (near my fundamental price target).

Ultimately, SWK has underperformed the broad market for years, so the onus is on the bulls to lift the stock from the current trading range.

SWK: Stuck In A Congestion Zone, Flat 200dma

The Bottom line

I have a "Buy" rating on SWK. The stock is cheap, given earnings growth and a high dividend yield. Bear in mind that this $12.2 billion market cap company is a dividend aristocrat, and tariff trouble may now largely be in the rearview mirror.

data.title: Tesla: The Future Is Promising, The Valuation Assumes It's Here
data.description:
Thesis

Tesla (TSLA) stock is always in the hot seat for investors abroad, touting a lofty valuation and perhaps loftier ambitions. I haven't covered this stock in quite some time, but today I'm going to break down why I still struggle to get behind Tesla's current valuation, even though I do believe the company's energy generation and storage segment, Terafab, and Optimus ventures could be a massive success story down the road. Let's dive in.

1Q26 Earnings

Tesla reported mixed 1Q26 earnings, with a miss on revenue and vehicle deliveries, and a beat on net income, earnings, and margin expansion. The revenue miss falls in line with my expectations, as the company's automotive performance has been cooling down and pricing headwinds for their energy generation and storage segment are likely to be setting in. While automotive sales were up 20% YoY, the annual trend has been on a downward trajectory. I'd like to see longer-term improvement in automotive sales before getting excited about those figures.

Energy generation and storage were cited to have fallen due to a decrease in Megapack and Powerwall deployments. Make of this what you will, but I believe this is a result of a cost-to-entry barrier that I mentioned previously. This doesn't change my take on the energy generation and storage segment long-term, as I believe the transition to alternative energy will bode well for Tesla looking forward. As expected, automotive regulatory credits were down 36% YoY, which I continue to believe will be problematic for Tesla. Bullish investors may combat this ideology with the fact that earnings beat and margins expanded; however, it is noted in Tesla's 10-Q that these figures came in higher than expected partly due to one-off warranty and tariff benefits.

Tesla's services and other figures are likely the highlight of the report, up 42% YoY. As Tesla continues to sell more vehicles, FSD sales, supercharging, maintenance, and insurance sales should continue to exhibit growth. In short, Tesla's 1Q26 earnings report is as expected for me, aside from some minor disappointment regarding energy generation and storage performance.

Near-term valuation doesn't change for me here either. Tesla is still boasting a P/E ratio of 341 and a forward P/E ratio of 178, both figures well above that of automotive peers and even other tech growth companies. Their P/FCF ratio is also at 199, with cash flow coming in much higher than expected, as investors have been anticipating cash flows to deteriorate with AI investing activities. Based on fiscal year-end figures for FY25, regulatory credits proved to be quite a liferaft for Tesla's bottom line figures.

The story hasn't changed when looking at 1Q26 figures either. First quarter net income and free cash flow came in at $477 million and $1.444 billion, respectively. Tesla reported regulatory credit income of $380 million. This means that 79.7% of Tesla's net income and 26.3% of free cash flow came from regulatory credit income. In case I didn't reiterate this previously, this is a big concern for two reasons: regulatory credit income carries no cost to produce, and they're set to expire in 2027. Tesla's 1Q26 results continue to demonstrate how reliant its bottom-line figures are on subsidies. With massive investing cash outflows and diminishing regulatory credit income on the horizon, I hope investors are ready to see some scary reports from Tesla moving forward. Tesla's 1Q26 results look like a continuation of what we saw between FY24 and FY25 with some minor variances.

Operational Overview (Fiscal Year-End)

The Good: Tesla's services and other segments have been a bright spot for the company. I particularly like the FSD sales/subscriptions. In fact, Tesla has ended its one-time purchase of its FSD software as of February 14th, 2026, aiming to transition to a subscription-only model. I do think this could work quite well for Tesla if they can capture enough Tesla owners. It's currently $100/month for FSD, which I think is a bit high.

We'll have to see how this segment performs under the subscription-only model moving forward. Tesla's services and other segments' total revenue has increased by roughly 50% since FY23 and is up 19% from FY24. Tesla did note that the 19% YoY increase wasn't driven by FSD revenue, so it will be interesting to see if they can scale FSD revenue. Regardless, a 50% jump in total revenue over a 2-year period is a positive sign. I'd like to note I didn't mention the potential for FSD licensing to other auto manufacturers, as we haven't seen any licensing deals come to fruition yet.

Tesla's energy generation and storage segment has been even more impressive, with FY25 total revenue of $12.771 billion. This figure is up 27% YoY, and more than 100% from FY23. The growth in this segment is undeniably impressive. Tesla's 1Q26 results didn't look as good here as I was hoping, but I'm still optimistic about this segment in the long term. Based on Tesla's FY25 10k, they are expected to realize $4.96 billion in deferred revenue throughout FY26, which is more than double the deferred revenue they realized in FY25. This does highlight a backlog in Megapack and Powerwall installations and leads me to believe we'll see solid 1Q26 figures for this segment.

The Bad: While it's not uncommon to see growth companies exhibit outlandish valuations, buying into said valuations can be a hard pill to swallow. Tesla is a bit of an anomaly, as they've solidified themselves in the EV automotive industry while keeping their sights on other, unique ventures like robotaxis, robots, and now a Terafab that could hold up to a $5 trillion price tag on the door. Nevertheless, investors either can't get enough of this stock or can't stay farther away from it. That said, let's look at the valuation.

Starting with our basic valuation metrics for FY25, Tesla ended the fiscal year with a P/E ratio of 416, with their forward P/E ratio also coming in at 222. Based on the following source, Tesla's forward P/E ratio is roughly 9x the current average P/E ratio of the top 60 automakers across the globe. I know Tesla investors argue that Tesla is so much more than just an automotive company. However, at this point in time, Tesla's auto sales are keeping the lights on.

While Tesla does deserve a premium compared to legacy automakers, current multiples imply it has already massively succeeded as a multi-industry enterprise, which isn't necessarily the case. This is reinforced by the fact that there isn't a clear breakdown of what percentage of the $15.487 billion in investment cash outflows were allocated to automotive, energy storage, robotics, and/or the Terafab project. We can't just assume that Tesla's cash flows are low because they're investing in other projects when that's not clearly outlined for us.

Cash flow is always a better indicator than earnings. In Tesla's case, their FY25 P/FCF Ratio was better than their P/E Ratio-coming in at 271-but still outlandish from a valuation perspective. Whether you look at the Dow, Nasdaq, or automotive industry, Tesla's P/FCF ratio is substantially higher than the average of any of them.

The Ugly: Let's wrap these metrics into operational performance and profitability. This is where things get even more concerning, in my opinion. Starting off, Tesla's automotive revenues have been declining since FY23. It's one thing to justify high valuations with rapid growth; it's another to buy into absurd valuations when the segment that accounts for 86.5% of total revenue is in a 2-year decline. I know we can look to find excuses for this performance, but BYD (BYDDY) increased total revenue by nearly 33% and surpassed Tesla as the global leading EV auto manufacturer by volume during the same respective 2-year period. While BYD has performed well, some of Tesla's automotive decline likely reflects broader EV market normalization.

Another metric that I find alarming when looking at Tesla's valuation is the relevance of regulatory credit income. Tesla's net income to common for FY25 came in at $3.794 billion, with free cash flow coming in at $6.22 billion. Their FY25 income from automotive regulatory credits came in at $1.993 billion. Keep in mind regulatory credit income doesn't carry a cost of revenue-it's revenue that goes straight to the bottom line.

Looking at this, we can see that Tesla's regulatory credit income accounted for 52.5% of their net income and 32% of their free cash flow. This is alarming because an extremely large portion of Tesla's earnings/cash flow is derived from credit revenue that is set to expire in 2027.
