If these regulatory credits disappear, we will likely see Tesla's earnings and cash flow drop, and margins will compress. Tesla's valuation is already hard to look at now; imagine what it will look like if regulatory credits get wiped off the books. While Tesla has some impressive growth segments in the works with its services and energy generation and storage, they're not enough to remotely compensate for the valuation we're seeing here.

My Take

Contrary to my critical insight into Tesla, I really like what they are doing as a company. Tesla has proven to be successful in the automotive industry; their battery generation and storage products are innovative and in high demand, and the FSD subscription model and potential for licensing could be massive. When added to projects not quite off the ground, like robotaxis, robotics, and the Terafab, I do think there is a lot of potential. However, Tesla appears to be priced as though everything they want to accomplish has been accomplished.

The automotive industry runs on thin margins; there's no way to justify having an automotive company with a $1.26 trillion market cap. Energy generation and storage are promising, but expensive for consumers and even enterprises to transition to. Legacy automakers are working endlessly to not just match Tesla's EV capabilities, but also their FSD technology. Adoption and licensing remain uncertain, making it difficult to justify pricing Tesla as a scaled software platform.

Tesla is effectively priced as though FSD has achieved full autonomy-an outcome that would transform the business into a high-margin software and robotaxi platform. However, that reality has not yet materialized. Tesla's potential is accompanied by no shortage of headwinds. I think many could still make the case that Tesla would be overvalued at $200/share, which would be a far more reasonable price while still carrying the valuation premiums of a growth stock.

The Terafab

While strategically compelling, Terafab has little bearing on Tesla's near- to mid-term valuation. With that said, I do like this project as a long-term development. Elon Musk has seemingly learned that there's no better way to make a billion dollars-or $800 billion-than get involved with the government. The Terafab project will be partnered with Intel (INTC), which has already received over $10 billion in CHIPS Act grants to manufacture chips domestically. Why? Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) currently holds a significant corner on chip manufacturing globally. TSMC currently produces over 50% of the world's microchips and over 90% of the world's most advanced chips.

Considering the global tensions between Russia and Ukraine, the U.S. and Iran, and China and Taiwan, it's certainly in the U.S.'s best interest to start manufacturing advanced microchips domestically. With that said, some analysts are estimating that the Terafab could cost as much as $5 trillion to bring to fruition. This price tag will take a lot more than series funding or an IPO to achieve. With the U.S.'s technological horizon at the will of Taiwan-a country at risk of invasion from China-I would be surprised if we didn't see significant government subsidization for the Terafab.

With absolutely everything that we do revolving around technology, microchips, data storage, RAM, DRAM, etc., I think the domestic semiconductor space is going to be a lucrative one to be in. According to Deloitte, semiconductor sales growth in FY25 reached 22% and is projected to jump to 26% through FY26. They even expect sales to achieve $2 trillion/year by FY36 with tapering sales growth. Companies manufacturing advanced AI chips will benefit the most from this expected growth, which is exactly what Terafab is aiming to do. I'd like to note that the Terafab really is a 'moonshot' in terms of a facility that can produce a terawatt of AI compute capacity annually.

While I'm confident that Tesla is going to make its way into the semiconductor industry, it's not going to have a factory that can produce 70% of TSMC's global production in a matter of a few years. However, Tesla doesn't need this massive of a factory to break into the semiconductor industry. This project could take 10 plus years and still prove to be a massive long-term win for Tesla and its investors.

Optimus

Tesla's Optimus robot may be one of the biggest products ever and the future of Tesla, according to Elon Musk himself. While we haven't seen any public sales of Optimus, the Gen 3 bot is slated to be released to the public by late 2027 with a price tag between $20,000 and $30,000. While that may seem quite high to some, I think it could be very achievable for many households. The Optimus Gen 3 is designed to be capable of cleaning a home, cooking, and performing other various household tasks.

Owning an Optimus could be equivalent to adding a relatively cheap car to the household budget. According to YCharts, there are roughly 133.68 million households in the U.S. alone. With about 52% of households considered middle-class, that means we have roughly 69.51 million households that could have the means to add an Optimus to the budget. Even at 50 million, we're just looking at the potential citizen household market. This means that Tesla is targeting a market that could see roughly $1.25 trillion in sales to just private households, going off an average price tag of $25 thousand sold to 50 million households. This doesn't even account for corporate/commercial or international purchases of Optimus. It also doesn't account for the revenue potential of servicing, upgrades, or possible insurance premiums for Optimus.

Considering that's more of a total addressable market view, even with 5 million unit sales, Tesla could add $125 billion to revenue. When looking at these figures, it's easy to see why Musk believes Optimus is the future of Tesla. Until we see Optimus come to the public market and what initial sales figures look like, it's hard to quantify this into Tesla's current valuation. Keep in mind these figures would take several years of adoption to be realized, don't account for future competition, regulation/liability headwinds, or potential demand elasticity headwinds. I'm simply using reasonable figures in terms of purchase-capable households and expected price points to show how big this market could be if captured. Regardless, Optimus does have massive potential and really could be one of the biggest products in history.

Valuation

I find it hard to value a company like Tesla because of its sporadicity. The reality is that Tesla Automotive is not performing exceptionally well. Tesla's services and other segment is heavily reliant on its automotive performance, and battery generation and storage will face headwinds. As for their in-work projects, they have a lot of potential, but we can't just blindly say they will all be a success story. I have generally believed that a 50x-100x multiple can be reasonable for promising growth stocks when it comes to earnings, cash flows, and predictability.

In the case of Tesla, I will use their 6-year average P/E and P/FCF ratios of roughly 230 and 215, respectively, because I am bullish on the company's vision, and these are well within the historical trading range for Tesla. These ratios would give Tesla an earnings value of $248.40/share and a free cash flow value of $378.40/share. I will take an average of these two figures, which brings us to $313.40/share. Keep in mind this value is based on a market-consistent valuation range given Tesla's historical trading behavior-not a fundamentals-based intrinsic value.

Conclusion

To wrap things up, I like where Tesla is headed a lot more than I ever have. I was always perplexed by the sentiment that Tesla's would be the most common car on the road in tandem with the idea that every vehicle would be utilizing their FSD technology. While Tesla does it best, there is far too much legacy competition to justify Tesla's stock valuation with only their automotive business in mind.

As the world continues to evolve, I do like their position in the energy generation and storage market, as well as leading the automotive supercharging network. Where my stance on Tesla has really taken a turn is with their Terafab and Optimus initiative. It's necessary for the U.S. to rid itself of its dependence on foreign semiconductors, primarily for purposes of national security.

With Intel already being incentivized to manufacture domestically in conjunction with their partnership with Terafab, I'm confident we will see large cash infusions from the government to get Terafab off the ground. We've all seen the success of semiconductors with the rise of technology, particularly AI as of recently, and I don't see it slowing down anytime soon. Optimus has significant potential as well, although the price may be hard for many consumers to justify.

In short, I believe energy generation/infrastructure, Tesla's move into the semiconductor industry, and Optimus will be the future of Tesla. While my overall stance on Tesla is bullish, I think the stock is a hold until you can get shares under $320/share. As a value investor, I had a hard time writing this take on a stock I haven't even considered for years; however, sometimes an old dog can learn new tricks. I like the direction that Tesla is headed, and the company may justify its valuation one day-but today, the valuation assumes that day has already arrived.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: POET: I Called The Surge, Now Reset
data.description:
Investment Thesis

POET Technologies (POET) has made a full cycle, from the high of Marvell (MRVL) driven hype, the shares have returned to their starting point, albeit with much more skepticism baked into the stock. That cycle is what I see as resetting expectations to make another speculative play feasible. I exited my position after a multibagger run to de-risk, not because I lost faith in the technology.

Since my last coverage, the stock is still up ~35% and briefly delivered a ~3x move, peaking at $15.5 in a very short time. What’s different now is the setup. Expectations are low, sentiment is damaged and valuation is anchored partly by its ~$430 million cash position. If the company executes and lands a credible customer, the upside can re-open quickly. It still is a speculative buy with a potential bottom forming but one that now depends entirely on proof, not narrative.

Marvell Incident: A Credibility Break, Not Just a Revenue Loss

POET lost the purchase order after publicly revealing its connection to Marvell, triggering a confidentiality breach that led Marvell to cancel the agreement. Management confirmed a connection to Marvell technology publicly in a retail-focused interview. This immediately kicked off a sharp run-up as the market interpreted it as Tier 1 confirmation. Yet within days, Marvell canceled the purchase order because of a breach of confidentiality agreement. The same information that sent the stock up by ~70% caused an almost 50% fall.

What makes the situation particularly ugly is the timing. POET knew of the termination of the contract but did not share it with the public and allowed the stock to keep rallying. Now, while this is obviously not good, that's a textbook setup that triggers investigations and lawsuits against management. POET was fortunate in a way that Marvell's response was relatively tame.

Tier 1 semiconductor suppliers take confidentiality seriously. If you get into trouble there, then your business is basically doomed. From this perspective, POET lost a deal and a lot more trust from the customer. Going forward recovery is possible but the standards have been raised significantly.

The Customer's Response Was Predictable

First things first. From Marvell's perspective POET fits into a much bigger picture which revolves around their AI interconnects and photonics technology. With the acquisition of Celestial AI, they got themselves one piece of the optical technology puzzle. So POET became part of the supply chain of a hyperscale provider and this comes with certain obligations.

Disclosure of any information from the company to outside parties can leak critical data such as product direction, deployment date or architectural plans. This means that when POET broke the non-disclosure agreement with Marvell, they were essentially putting their partner at risk.

Terminating the deal with POET was not an emotional decision, it was a necessity to prevent leakage. In terms of business relationships that's completely understandable and expected.

A Platform Built to Solve the Right Problem

Without the buzz around POET, one would easily identify the genuine challenge that the company is addressing. Specifically, as the AI clusters grow exponentially in size, it quickly becomes clear that the power consumption, latency and bandwidth constraints will force the industry to use optics for connecting devices. In short, POET appears to be positioning itself inside of the inevitable wave.

To put things into context, the firm's Optical Interposer platform is designed to address the challenges with conventional electrical interconnects by using automated processes to integrate photonic modules at the wafer level. Put another way, the company aims to industrialize the optical module manufacturing process, just like semiconductors industrialized the compute process decades ago.

Obviously, there is no guarantee that POET's solution will work and scale, but the platform itself does seem to offer an important advantage. Specifically, by offering wafer-scale manufacturing capabilities of photonics and electronics and being substrate agnostic, the platform addresses the primary cost constraint in deploying 1.6T and potentially, 3.2T AI clusters.

Furthermore, the growing adoption of optics among the key players in the semiconductor and computing space provides yet another piece of evidence that POET's approach makes sense. Indeed, if you look at the industry direction and the level of investments in photonics, co-packaged optics and optical circuit switching, it is clear that demand is building up fast.

Therefore, it would be safe to say that while POET's technology may not drive the adoption of optics in the data center, it certainly aligns with the industry direction.

Valuation: Back to Base But With a Trust Discount

POET has re-traced all the way down to its pre-Marvell valuation of $7-$8 level. While it would be wrong to call this a clean reset, it certainly lost some hype premium but also got a credibility discount. Previously, the company was valued as a pre-scale, very early-stage photonics play. Now it is the same play, but with the added governance concerns that will matter for institutional buyers. It totally changes the valuation paradigm.

The $430 million in cash provides downside protection and a legitimate customer win could trigger a dramatic re-rating. However, here we are talking about the execution risk, not narrative risk. This becomes apparent from looking into valuation multiples, which show that while POET may look cheap on a current basis, it is still expensive on a forward basis, considering the lack of clear revenue upside visibility.

This is definitely not a conviction investment but a conditional opportunity. It is a starter position, to say the least. The catalyst in this case is not going to be valuation re-valuation, nor the technical support levels. Catalyst would be the delivery of a legitimate commercial success.

While POET could have put in a short term bottom, the numbers indicate that it's more of a technical bottom rather than a confirmed bottom. With the share price around $8, the company is valued at about a $1.1 billion market cap versus ~$430 million cash on hand, so 40% of its valuation comes from cash holdings alone and this makes sense because despite crashing from $15.10 to $7.95 (-47%) in one day, the company held above the low of $7.54 in intraday trading.

Volumes reveal everything. Over 80 million shares traded versus an average of about 10 million, which often indicates capitulation and this usually forms a bottom due to forced selling. Therefore, I believe there's a possibility that POET might be forming a tradable bottom here. However, without any catalysts, it seems more like technical exhaustion with some cash support rather than conviction.

Lawsuits Are The Least Of It, Trust Is Broken

Obviously, from the pure stock investment perspective, the most immediate threat is the lawsuit. However, there are other, more structural factors as well. For instance, after this incident, POET will probably find it much harder to secure new partnerships with Tier 1 customers. Indeed, the company will no longer appear as a reliable partner that maintains confidentiality, which is one of the keys to successful cooperation.

Takeaway

POET is not technologically broken but now it’s all about execution and not about vision anymore. Credibility and trust have been compromised to a point where we are talking about proof-of-concepts through execution and nothing else.

data.title: Pollard Banknote: A High-Probability Bet On Margin Recovery
data.description:
Introduction

Pollard Banknote (PBL:CA) is not a name that shows up in most investors. It’s a small/mid-cap Canadian company that doesn’t have a big institutional following as it doesn’t fit squarely in any one sector category. But since I’ve been covering the name for almost 2 years, I think the business has continued to look more attractive and could be ripe for a takeout candidate given the valuation now.

Last month, the company reported another solid quarter to close out FY’25, capping a record revenue year while absorbing some deliberate and, in my view, temporary margin pressure from its push into higher-value digital platforms. The lottery and gaming services space isn't glamorous, but Pollard has built a business with long-duration contracted revenue, pricing power in its core instant ticket segment, and a growing digital platform business that I think the market is undervaluing. With some of the heavier investment spending now in the rearview mirror, it would seem to me that this year is shaping up to be a cleaner and more obvious earnings story.

Recent Results

Pollard Banknote reported Q4’25 revenue of $150.8 million, up from $140.3 million in the prior year period but missing slightly by $0.3 million on sellside estimates. Including the company's 50% share of NeoPollard Interactive, combined sales reached $185 million, a 10% increase year over year. For the full year, revenues were up 7.0% to $596.0 million.

To unpack what drove revenue growth, it’s helpful to unpack it by revenue drivers. This quarter, higher average instant ticket selling prices and an improved proprietary mix contributed $1.4 million, while growth in other lottery products and services added $2.8 million, driven largely by the successful Kansas iLottery launch on the Catalyst platform. The Pacific Gaming acquisition added $2.1 million in charitable volume gains, stronger Michigan iLottery sales contributed $1.3 million boosted by large jackpots, and favorable FX tailwinds added another $4.5 million. FY’25 was a record year for revenue for the company thanks to contract wins and an ongoing mix shift toward higher-value digital and technology solutions. As I’ll discuss later in the article, that shift in mix has hurt the company in the short term but I think it’ll be the right one longer term and we should see that flow through on the bottom line in FY’26.

On the contract front, Pollard secured the primary instant ticket supply role with the California Lottery under a 12-year agreement, won a major 12-year central gaming system contract with the Belgium National Lottery valued at approximately CAD $289 million, and landed a seven-year Oklahoma Lottery loyalty, website, app and CRM deal worth roughly USD $10 million. On the earnings call, management noted that they extended the North Carolina contract to October 2028, and expanded its game library to over 145 titles across nine jurisdictions. These wins matter because they underpin multi-year revenue visibility that I think the market isn't fully appreciating yet.

Profitability showed some pressure but for largely anticipated reasons. Gross profit held at $22.4 million in Q4 though the margin eased to 14.9% from 16.0% in the prior year, primarily due to Kansas iLottery start-up costs and regulatory pressure on Minnesota eTabs. Further down, EBITDA rose 10% to $27.7 million and was helped by a $4.3 million increase in the NeoPollard equity pickup to $16.9 million.

In the small chart I put together below, we can see that despite the revenue and profitability growth that there has been some margin compression, about 210bps on gross profit and 40bps on EBITDA. But I think what the market is missing is that this was largely due to a heavier investment cycle and the underlying instant ticket segment has largely recovered to historical contribution levels through successful repricing. Going forward, I think margins can move a fair bit higher.

On the balance sheet, the company funded more than $58 million in capex during 2025 that included covering specialized presses, expanded game content, ICON eTab machines, and Catalyst development, entirely through internal operating cash flow. That level of self-funded investment while still delivering these financial results is, to me, a sign of a well-run business with real earnings power underneath the near-term noise. With total debt at $197.5 million and a cash position of $28.5 million, gross leverage sits at 1.65x and net leverage sits at 1.40x.

Expect margin expansion from here

On the conference call following the quarter, Pollard Banknote did not issue formal quantitative guidance for 2026. That’s pretty consistent with how management has historically communicated but the earnings call gave me a reasonably clear picture of where the year is headed and it seems more positive than the lack of hard numbers might suggest.

The two biggest headwinds in 2025, the Kansas iLottery ramp and the Minnesota eTab pressure, both appear to be turning. Management was clear about this on the call with Co-CEO John Pollard noting that Q1 on both of those factors "should be significantly better than Q4," which tells me the margin recovery is already underway rather than being a back-half story that investors have to take on faith. Kansas is expected to move toward profitability by the second half of 2026, with the drag steadily shrinking throughout the year. Minnesota eTabs, where regulatory changes hurt the entire supplier ecosystem in 2025, is expected to exceed 2024 play levels for the full year as the company actively improves its game content to compete more effectively in that market.

On gross margins more broadly, management seems to see no reason why the business can't move back toward the historical low-20s range throughout 2026, driven by the instant ticket repricing that has already largely taken hold, the Kansas improvement, and the eTab recovery. That's a meaningful step up from the 14.9% reported in Q4, and I think it's achievable given the trajectory already underway. Keep in mind that FY’24 gross margins were also just shy of 19% so a 20% gross margin isn’t out of question. With the absence of one-time ERP and integration costs that weighed on 2025 ($3.1 million, as shown below), it should also make for a cleaner comparison as the year progresses.

The California contract is another important piece of the 2026 story. Doubling market share from 30% to 70% with the largest U.S. lottery is a significant volume step-up. Perhaps more importantly, Pollard Banknote has confirmed that existing printing capacity can absorb this without meaningful incremental CapEx. That means the revenue upside from California flows through at attractive incremental margins. Belgium is a longer-dated ramp but adds to the contracted backlog that underpins the multi-year revenue picture.

What to expect next quarter and for the balance of the year

For Q1 2026, I think the setup is reasonably constructive. Management's comment that both Kansas and Minnesota should improve significantly relative to Q4 is the key thing to watch, and I'll be looking for gross margin to start recovering toward that low-20s target range.

As shown below, analysts are expecting revenue of $153.75 million, EBITDA of $31.4 million, and EPS of $0.42 (source: Bloomberg). Given that management has explicitly flagged significant sequential improvement in both Kansas and Minnesota, I think these estimates are reasonable and possibly conservative on EBITDA, particularly if the margin recovery is coming through as quickly as the tone on the call implied.

The Michigan iLottery rolloff on July 1 is an offset worth acknowledging, but I think the broader mix shift toward higher-value platform work more than compensates over time. And with total capex spend expected to be lower in 2026 now that the heavy lifting on Catalyst and ICON is largely complete, the free cash flow picture improves meaningfully from here.The sellside has modeled FCF increasing from $57.0 million in the LTM period to $153.3 million by the end of the decade, which is a 2.7x increase despite single digit revenue growth (source: Bloomberg).

Risks to consider

The most obvious near-term risk is execution on the Kansas iLottery ramp. The contract is strategically important, but it's still in early days and any delays in moving toward profitability could extend the margin headwind longer than expected. Minnesota eTabs are similarly a watch item given the regulatory dynamics at play there.

On the contract side, the Michigan iLottery rolloff in July is a revenue headwind that the business needs to absorb, and while I think the broader mix shift offsets it over time, it will create some noise in the year-over-year comparisons in the back half. Partner concentration is also worth keeping in mind since a meaningful portion of revenue runs through a relatively small number of lottery relationships. Finally, given that a portion of Pollard's business is reported in Canadian dollars but generated in the U.S. mostly, the FX has been a tailwind in the last two years but could cut both ways if the Canadian dollar strengthens.

Valuation and wrap up

Pollard Banknote’s valuation has completely derated from its highs in 2021 at the 45x level to 8.5x earnings today. When I initially rated the company has a buy in December 2024, the stock was being priced at 11.3x earnings. At a 20%+ discount to the valuation today, investors can buy a growing business that’s likely worth paying up for.

As I mentioned earlier, Pollard Banknote is pretty underfollowed by both retail and institutional buyers. The reason for that is the tight float. As shown below, the Pollard family owns roughly 64% of the company’s total shares outstanding. In my eyes, that’s a double-edged sword since it indicates skin in the game (as opposed to being incentivized through large bonuses, etc) but it does mean that PBL is less likely to be acquired since the Pollard family will largely determine whether any transaction goes through or not. I wasn’t able to find out there ages (the two co-CEOs) but they are both approaching retirement years. John Pollard joined the company in 1986 and Gordon Pollard joined in 1989. They both became Co-CEOs in 1997.

So while a takeover may be several years away, I do think the valuation is attractive for a growing business. Of the 4 analysts that cover the stock, they would probably agree given that the average price target is 89% above current trading levels.

Ultimately, Pollard Banknote is coming out of an intentional investment cycle, and I think the setup heading into 2026 is more interesting today than I did two years ago. The record 2025 revenue, the California market share win, the growing pipeline of long-duration contracted digital platform work, and the improving trajectory in both Kansas and Minnesota all point to a business that is starting to improve earnings. With capex moderating, margins expected to recover toward historical levels, and the free cash flow picture improving, I think 2026 represents the year where the investment thesis starts to pay off in a way that's hard to ignore. For investors willing to look past what was a noisy 2025, I believe Pollard offers a compelling combination of contracted revenue visibility and an improving earnings profile at an undemanding valuation.

data.title: Aurora Innovation: Autonomous Trucking Nears Commercial Inflection
data.description:
Aurora Innovation's (AUR) stock appears to have found a floor over the past 6 months as momentum in the underlying business begins to build. I previously suggested that Aurora was making material progress in its attempt to scale driverless freight operations. This has continued in recent months, with driverless miles and deployed capabilities both increasing. While the early signs are positive, it will be late 2026 at the earliest before Aurora really demonstrates its ability to scale driverless operations though.

Aurora's stock has performed poorly since listing, but the next 12 months could be more constructive due to the accelerated deployment of driverless trucks. It will likely will be 2028 before Aurora's revenue multiple begins to look more reasonable, although this matters little, as the company has a long growth runway and just needs to demonstrate its ability to scale.

Market Conditions

While macro uncertainty is elevated at the moment, and diesel prices could cause disruptions to the freight industry, Aurora's macro exposure is limited at the moment due to a lack of scale. A severe downturn could create caution amongst potential customers, but Aurora's growth is likely to remain dictated by supply rather than demand.

On the supply side, Aurora is making progress with its manufacturing partnerships and continues to expand its operating envelope. The company has suggested that its addressable market is now over 3.6 billion vehicle miles.

Aurora Innovation

Aurora surpassed 250,000 driverless miles in January, suggesting that the company's driverless operations have continued to ramp in recent months. It is unclear how many driverless vehicles the company now has on the road, but Aurora was previously targeting 10 driverless vehicles by the end of 2025. It is also worth noting that Aurora still has an observer in its vehicles due to partner requirements. Aurora is now targeting 200 driverless trucks in operation by the end of 2026 with deployment weighted to the back half. While this would already represent a step change in scale, industrialized scaling isn't expected until 2027.

Growth is supported by the fact that the Aurora Driver is now capable of driverless operations in a range of inclement weather (rain, fog and heavy wind). While this may seem like a fairly minor update, Aurora estimates that it was unable to operate around 40% of the time in 2025 due to weather conditions. The company continues to invest in inclement weather capabilities, and is collecting data in the Rockies so that it can eventually deploy its trucks in snowy conditions.

Aurora's latest software release also expands driverless operations to multiple new lanes, including El Paso to Fort Worth. In addition, Aurora is now conducting supervised autonomous operations between Dallas and Laredo, which is an important freight route for trade between the US and Mexico. Aurora also believes that its system is now sufficiently generalized to enable expansion across the Sunbelt.

Aurora is also making meaningful progress automating the creation of new content for the Aurora Atlas. Aurora's Verifiable AI system is used to automatically generate semantic components of the Aurora Atlas, significantly reducing human labor requirements. As a result, the expansion of the Aurora Atlas is expected to accelerate going forward.

Aurora is hauling sand 20 hours a day for Detmar. Most of the 60-mile route is on the highway though, making this a significantly different operation to Kodiak's frac sand hauling. Detmar is expected to transition to driverless vehicles in Q2 when Aurora deploys its second fleet of driverless trucks. The company also has plans to leverage its technology in areas like agriculture and mining, drones.

On the manufacturing front, the first Volvo autonomous trucks recently rolled off the pilot line, supporting deployment at scale. Aurora's second-generation hardware kit is also expected in the next few months. The cost of the components in this kit will be roughly halved, while durability is expected to triple. This hardware will also enable driverless operations without an observer.

Financial Analysis

Aurora generated roughly 3 million USD revenue in 2025 which was weighted to the back half of the year, with fourth quarter revenue increasing 25% sequentially to 1 million USD.

Aurora expects 14-16 million USD revenue in 2026, which will be heavily back-loaded. The company is also targeting an 80 million USD revenue rate exiting 2026 from a fleet of more than 200 driverless vehicles (no observer).

Initial revenue comes from a Transportation-as-a-Service model, with Aurora transitioning to a Driver-as a-Service model in 2027. Under TaaS, Aurora owns the fleet and provides the freight service. In comparison, customers own the fleet in the DaaS model with Aurora providing the self-driving technology. Aurora expects its TaaS fleet to remain below 500 trucks.

Aurora's operating loss totaled 238 million USD in Q4 while operating cash burn was 146 million USD and CapEx was 8 million USD. Despite this, Aurora's liquidity is still almost 1.5 billion USD which the company believes will be sufficient for it to reach breakeven in 2028.

Quarterly cash burn is expected to be in the 190-220 million USD range on average in 2026 and Aurora is targeting breakeven gross margins on a run rate basis exiting the year. While reduced hardware costs will help with this, Aurora also needs to reduce the burn of remote assistance and on-site support. It is unclear where the company's current performance sits, but it is likely in the high cost part of the curves below. For comparison, Pony has suggested that it is on target to achieve 1 remote assistant for every 30 vehicles, although the time frame for this isn't clear.

Conclusion

Pressure on SaaS stocks in 2026 demonstrate that the market is starting to more broadly price in the impact of AI. Self-driving vehicle companies are yet to really benefit from this though, which is somewhat surprising given that operations are scaling.

There are limited options to get clean and direct exposure to self-driving technology at the moment. Stocks like Alphabet (GOOG), Tesla (TSLA) and Mobileye (MBLY) are driven in large part by their non-self-driving businesses, and Chinese companies like Pony and WeRide expose investors to significant risk, independent of business fundamentals.

As a result, Aurora is perhaps one of the best ways to gain exposure to the proliferation of self-driving vehicles that is now happening. 2026 will be make or break in terms of the company's ability to scale driverless operations, and success would likely see the stock move significantly higher.

While Aurora is becoming an interesting opportunity, uncertainty regarding how the autonomous trucking will evolve remains high. There are a number of reasonably well resourced competitors that are all at similar stages of maturity. In addition, Waymo and Tesla are likely to target the freight market in time. This could be problematic as competition will largely be based on price and I don't expect there to be a meaningful first mover advantage.
